Fintech • Developer Platform • Web Console

Bigul API Connect

A self-serve developer console that replaced a manual, support-driven API key request process with transparent, secure self-service for algo-traders and third-party integrators.

Role

UI/UX Designer
(API Platform Ownership)

Duration

Feb 2025 - Apr 2025
(3 Month Sprint)

Team Size

5 cross-functional members
(1 PM, 3 Devs, 1 Security Engineer)

Key Results

−30% API Support Tickets
< 2 min Self-Serve Key Setup

ON THIS PAGE

1. Project Overview

Background & Business Context

As Bigul's retail trading base matured, a growing segment of power users wanted programmatic access to their own accounts — to build personal trading bots, connect third-party charting tools, or automate portfolio rebalancing. Every modern brokerage eventually faces this fork: keep trading data locked inside your own app, or open a controlled API layer and let a developer ecosystem grow around your platform.

Bigul chose to open up, but the existing process for getting API access was entirely manual: a user emailed support, a backend engineer manually generated a key, and it was shared back over email with no audit trail, no scoping, and no way for the user to see how much of their rate limit they'd consumed until they hit a wall.

Goals & Objectives:

  • Replace manual key issuance with a self-serve console requiring no support intervention.
  • Make permissions explicit so users only grant the access their integration actually needs.
  • Give developers real-time visibility into usage and rate limits before they get throttled.

Target Audience

Two distinct developer segments used this console very differently:

Retail Power Users (72%)

Hobbyist coders building personal trading bots or connecting to charting tools like Amibroker. Comfortable with APIs but not security specialists.

Integration Partners (28%)

Small fintech teams building products on top of Bigul's infrastructure, needing higher rate limits, webhook support, and audit-grade logging.

2. My Role & Collaboration

I owned the end-to-end UX for the console — from the security model's user-facing implications through to the final visual design of every screen state.

This was the most security-sensitive interface I'd designed at Bigul, so collaboration was tighter than usual:

  • Security Engineering: Translating key-scoping, encryption-at-rest, and audit-log requirements into a permission model regular developers could actually understand without reading documentation first.
  • Backend Team: Aligning on what rate-limit data could be surfaced in real time versus what would require polling, which directly shaped the usage dashboard's refresh design.
  • Product Management: Positioning API access as a future monetizable platform tier, which meant designing the free tier's constraints to feel like a fair starting point, not an artificial limitation.
  • Developer Relations: Making sure every UI label matched the terminology already used in the public API documentation, so users never had to mentally translate between the console and the docs.

3. Research & Discovery

I audited how established brokers (Zerodha's Kite Connect, Alpaca, Interactive Brokers) present API access, and ran short remote interviews with five active Bigul users who had previously emailed support requesting API keys.

Competitive Benchmarking

Platform Key Issuance Time Usage Visibility Self-Serve Revoke
Legacy Broker A 3-5 business days (email) None No (support only)
Modern API-First Broker B Instant (self-serve) Real-time dashboard Yes, one click
Bigul (Pre-redesign) 2-5 business days (email) None No (support only)

User Persona & Key Pain Points

Meet Priya, a 29-year-old data analyst who builds trading bots on weekends. She represents our largest API user segment:

01
The Multi-Day Wait

Priya would email support for a key and wait days, often losing momentum on a weekend side project by the time access finally arrived.

02
Invisible Rate Limits

Her bot would silently start failing when she hit an undocumented rate limit, with no dashboard telling her how close she was until it broke.

03
All-or-Nothing Access

The one key she received had full trading permissions, even though her bot only needed to read market data — a real security exposure she was uncomfortable with.

Product Thinking Insight:

The support tickets weren't really about access speed — they were a symptom of a missing self-service layer. Every ticket represented a developer who was ready to build immediately, but the platform had no path for that except a human in the loop.

4. Problem Definition

The Challenges & Constraints

Security and compliance requirements were non-negotiable: keys had to be encrypted at rest, every generation and revocation event needed an immutable audit log entry, and 2FA had to gate any key-generation action. The challenge was building a self-serve flow around those constraints without making it feel like a bureaucratic obstacle course.

-30% Support ticket target
< 2 min Self-serve setup goal
100% Audit trail coverage

5. Design Process

I mapped the full lifecycle of a key — generation, scoping, active use, and rotation or revocation — before drawing a single screen, since a security-sensitive flow needed the state model settled first.

Fig 1. Key lifecycle flow from generation through scoping, monitoring, and rotation.

From Low-Fidelity Wireframes to Iterations

The first wireframe presented every available permission scope with a single "Select All" checkbox at the top. In testing, six of eight participants clicked "Select All" by default — the opposite of least-privilege access we needed to encourage.

Fig 2. Wireframe iteration removing the "Select All" shortcut in favor of risk-tiered, individually confirmed scopes.
Design Rationale:

Removing the shortcut: We removed "Select All" entirely and grouped scopes into risk tiers (Read-Only, Order Placement, Fund Movement), requiring an extra tap to expand and confirm the higher-risk tiers. Setup took slightly longer, but over-permissioned keys dropped sharply in the beta.

6. Visual Design System

API Connect extended Bigul's existing Apex design system with a "developer console" density mode, rather than inventing a new visual language from scratch.

Fig 3. Apex extension covering monospace key display, scope-tier chips, and status indicators.

Design Tokens Overview

Typography

JetBrains Mono for key values and code snippets so characters like "0", "O", "1", and "l" are always visually distinct.

Status Color Coding

Active (emerald), Expiring within 7 days (amber), Revoked (muted grey) — each paired with an icon, never color alone.

Risk-Tier Chips

Read-only scopes use a calm blue chip; fund-movement scopes use a warning-red outline to visually flag higher-stakes permissions.

WCAG 2.1 Accessibility Considerations:

  • Copy Actions: Every key or token value ships with a labeled "Copy" button, so keyboard and screen-reader users never depend on a manual select gesture.
  • Status Independence: Key status is always conveyed with a text label and icon, never color alone, so color-blind developers can read state at a glance.

7. High-Fidelity Walkthrough

The Scoped Key Generator

Instead of a blanket access toggle, users build a key by explicitly opting into individual capabilities — read positions, place orders, cancel orders — each shown with a one-line explanation of what it allows.

The Solution: A risk-tiered accordion where read-only scopes are visible by default and higher-risk scopes require an explicit expand-and-confirm step, keeping the safest option the path of least resistance.

Live Usage Dashboard

Developers previously had no idea how close they were to a rate limit until their integration broke. We built a real-time consumption bar tied to each key.

The Solution: A simple horizontal usage meter with a proactive warning state at 80% of quota, plus a one-tap request for a temporary limit increase — turning a silent failure into a visible, actionable signal.

8. Product Strategy & Trade-offs

Opening a trading API means constantly weighing developer convenience against platform risk. A few trade-offs defined this project.

Convenience vs. Security Default

The Conflict: Developers wanted long-lived keys with broad access so they wouldn't have to reconfigure their bots often. Security wanted short-lived, narrowly scoped keys as the default.

The Strategy: Keys default to a 90-day expiry with the minimum scopes the user selects, but power users can extend expiry through an additional 2FA step. Convenience is available on request — it is never the silent default.

MVP Phase vs Future Roadmap

The MVP shipped scoped key generation and the live usage dashboard. The roadmap includes webhook event subscriptions for order-fill notifications and a sandbox environment so developers can test integrations against fake market data before going live.

9. Challenges & Solutions

Challenge: Beta users kept regenerating lost keys

The Problem: Following security best practice, we showed each new key's secret exactly once and never again. Beta testers who lost that value were regenerating keys constantly — creating the very support burden we set out to eliminate.

Design Iteration: We added a clearly labeled reminder step encouraging developers to store the key in their own secrets manager during the one-time reveal, plus a prominent one-click "Rotate Key" action so a lost key became a 10-second fix instead of a support ticket.

The Result: Key-loss-related support contact dropped sharply within the first month of the beta.

10. Impact & Results

The redesigned console delivered measurable gains within the first quarter of launch:

-30% API Support Tickets
< 2 min Key Setup Time
100% Audit Trail Coverage

Before vs. After Comparison

Previous Experience
  • 2-5 day manual key issuance via email
  • Full-access keys with no scoping
  • No usage or rate-limit visibility
  • Revocation required a support ticket
Redesigned Experience
  • Under 2 minutes, fully self-serve
  • Explicit, risk-tiered scoped permissions
  • Real-time usage dashboard with alerts
  • One-click rotate and revoke

11. Tools & Tech

Figma
FigJam
Miro
Postman
Notion
Jira

12. Design Gallery

Click on any of the screens to open a full-resolution interactive lightbox preview.

13. Key Learnings

Designing for developers instead of retail investors shifted a few of my assumptions about what "simple" actually means.

01
The safest default must also be the easiest one

Removing "Select All" wasn't about restricting users — it was about making the secure choice the path of least resistance, instead of relying on people to opt into safety.

02
Design for the fear of losing access, not just gaining it

The one-click rotate action solved more support tickets than any onboarding tooltip could, because it addressed what users were actually anxious about: being locked out.

03
Developer tools still need visual hierarchy

Treating a console as "just data" is a mistake — status color coding and risk-tier chips did as much work here as any consumer-facing onboarding screen.
